message:
chore(beads): sync issue tracker state

Update .beads/issues.jsonl with issue status transitions from recent
development sessions including FCP3 re-foundation work.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -729,7 +729,7 @@
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku","title":"[FCP3] Asupersync-Native Re-foundation: Spec V3, Runtime Kernel, and Secure Connector Operating Model","description":"# [FCP3] Asupersync-Native Re-foundation

## Goal
Re-found Flywheel Connector Protocol as an Asupersync-native secure connector operating system whose specification, runtime model, host, SDK, manifests, transports, and conformance story all describe the same reality.

## Why this exists
The current repository already contains serious FCP2/FCP2.5 work plus a partial Asupersync migration, but the architecture is still split between an ambitious security/capability model and a largely compatibility-oriented async/control-plane implementation. That split is strategically wrong. Asupersync is not just a faster executor or a better runtime dependency; it is the right operational model for FCP itself.

## Non-negotiable design decisions
- **No backwards compatibility at all.** We have zero users and do not want shims, adapters, dual stacks, compatibility modes, translators, or prolonged hybrid operation.
- **Spec-first re-foundation.** `FCP_Specification_V3.md` must become the primary source of truth and should read as if the platform was always designed this way.
- **Asupersync-native semantics.** Zone binding, capability narrowing, provenance, approval scope, deadlines, budgets, cancellation, supervision, drain, quiescence, and failure escalation must be first-class runtime semantics, not just fields on request structs.
- **Connectors are supervised apps.** A connector is not merely an async trait object behind a newline JSON-RPC loop; it is a supervised application with explicit topology, lifecycle, state, and transport boundaries.
- **Mechanical security.** No ambient authority, no hidden escape hatches, no policy enforced only by prompts or tribal knowledge.
- **Clean cutover.** Legacy compatibility surfaces should be removed in place once replacements exist; do not widen the compatibility perimeter.

## Program scope
This epic covers:
- specification V3 rewrite
- target architecture and crate graph redesign
- replacement of the compatibility substrate with a native FCP execution kernel
- host redesign as a supervised Asupersync application
- new host/connector ABI and transport model
- connector SDK V3 and manifest V3
- policy/sandbox/placement re-binding to runtime context
- conformance, forensics, performance, and pilot vertical slices
- final cutover and repository-wide documentation alignment

## Success criteria
- The repository has a coherent FCP3 bead graph that can be executed without referring back to an external markdown plan.
- The eventual implementation can delete rather than preserve legacy compatibility surfaces.
- The spec, README, AGENTS guidance, host, SDK, connectors, and conformance harness all converge on one operational model.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note
This bead is part of the FCP3 re-foundation and must remain self-contained. Future implementers should not need to consult the original planning conversation to understand intent, scope, architectural posture, or why the work matters.

## No-compat rule
Do not preserve backwards compatibility, dual-stack behavior, fallback paths, or migration-era abstractions beyond the minimum temporary quarantine explicitly called out elsewhere in the graph. Once a replacement path is proven, the old path should be deleted.

## Verification and evidence mandate
- Planning/spec/design beads must explicitly define the downstream verification story rather than hand-wave it.
- Implementation beads are not complete without focused unit/property tests, negative/failure-path coverage, and where relevant failure-injection coverage for cancellation, drain, restart, timeout, backpressure, failover, and recovery behavior.
- Host- or runtime-crossing changes must include end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logging, timestamps, digests/ids, receipts or health outcomes, and clear replay instructions.
- Evidence must be redaction-safe, easy to inspect, and sufficient for a future engineer to diagnose failures without re-deriving the intended behavior.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:52:51.505704Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:05:17.819182Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","fcp3","redesign","spec"]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1","title":"[FCP3] Specification V3 Program: rewrite the protocol around the Asupersync-native operating model","description":"# [FCP3] Specification V3 Program

## Goal
Create `FCP_Specification_V3.md` as the normative source of truth for the Asupersync-native FCP platform.

## Background
The current V2 specification describes a sophisticated secure mesh system, but the redesign requires more than edits around the edges. We need a document that treats supervised applications, explicit runtime context, operational manifests, framed host/connector control-data planes, and no-compat cutover as the baseline architecture.

## Scope
- clone `FCP_Specification_V2.md` to `FCP_Specification_V3.md`
- rewrite V3 so it reads as greenfield truth rather than a migration narrative
- fold in the Asupersync-native execution model across host, connector, ABI, manifests, policy, placement, and testing
- remove compatibility-oriented language that would imply a need for adapters or dual-stack operation

## Deliverables
- a rewritten V3 spec
- appendices/examples/checklists consistent with FCP3-only reality
- a requirements trace that lets downstream implementation beads reference the spec without ambiguity","notes":"## FCP3 execution note
This bead is part of the FCP3 re-foundation and must remain self-contained. Future implementers should not need to consult the original planning conversation to understand intent, scope, architectural posture, or why the work matters.

## No-compat rule
Do not preserve backwards compatibility, dual-stack behavior, fallback paths, or migration-era abstractions beyond the minimum temporary quarantine explicitly called out elsewhere in the graph. Once a replacement path is proven, the old path should be deleted.

## Verification and evidence mandate
- Planning/spec/design beads must explicitly define the downstream verification story rather than hand-wave it.
- Implementation beads are not complete without focused unit/property tests, negative/failure-path coverage, and where relevant failure-injection coverage for cancellation, drain, restart, timeout, backpressure, failover, and recovery behavior.
- Host- or runtime-crossing changes must include end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logging, timestamps, digests/ids, receipts or health outcomes, and clear replay instructions.
- Evidence must be redaction-safe, easy to inspect, and sufficient for a future engineer to diagnose failures without re-deriving the intended behavior.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:52:51.646473Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:05:17.687614Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["asupersync","fcp3","spec"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:52:51.646473Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.1","title":"[FCP3.SPEC] Source audit and gap map across V2 spec, README, code, and Asupersync","description":"Completed source audit and gap map for drafting `FCP_Specification_V3.md`.

Headline judgment:
- V2 already contains the right sovereignty, policy, provenance, manifest, registry, placement, audit, and offline-resilience ambitions.
- README and AGENTS reinforce the correct product posture: secure-by-default, aggressively automated, self-contained connector distribution, and zero interest in backwards compatibility.
- The implementation is still transitional. `fcp-core` carries rich domain semantics, but `fcp-async-core`, `fcp-sdk`, `fcp-host`, and many connectors still center `ExecutionContext`, `ConnectorRuntime`, Tokio compatibility, `reqwest`, and newline JSON-RPC over stdio.
- Asupersync supplies the missing semantic runtime. V3 should be written as if FCP has always been an Asupersync-native secure connector operating system, not a Tokio-era protocol retrofitted later.

This bead now captures the source hierarchy, V2-to-V3 section mapping, code-backed retire/reframe/keep/new lists, and drafting rules needed for `flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.2`.","design":"## Completed audit

### 1. Executive judgment

FCP3 should preserve the strongest ideas already present in V2, but it must reorganize the specification around the real operational center of gravity:
- explicit authority,
- zone and provenance enforcement,
- durable evidence and audit,
- portable connector execution,
- supervised lifecycle,
- budgeted and cancel-correct runtime semantics,
- deterministic verification.

The major architectural problem in the current source set is not that the ideas are weak. It is that the truth is split:
- `FCP_Specification_V2.md` is highly ambitious and already describes mesh, policy, checkpoint, lease, migration, audit, provenance, and supply-chain semantics.
- `README.md` tells a slightly simplified story centered on secure connector execution and digital sovereignty.
- the codebase still exposes transitional seams built around Tokio-era expectations and line-oriented JSON-RPC subprocess control.
- Asupersync already models the runtime semantics that FCP actually wants, but those semantics are not yet the normative core of the spec.

The V3 rewrite should therefore do three things at once:
1. Preserve the enduring domain model from V2.
2. Replace transitional compatibility language with Asupersync-native runtime semantics.
3. Reframe mesh/object/symbol machinery as a substrate serving execution, evidence, placement, portability, and resilience rather than as the only lens through which the entire system is introduced.

### 2. Source hierarchy for drafting V3

When `FCP_Specification_V3.md` is written, use sources in this priority order:

1. Enduring normative invariants already present in V2 and reflected in `fcp-core` / `fcp-manifest`.
2. Asupersync runtime semantics and application model (`Cx`, regions, scopes, budgets, outcomes, supervision, quiescence, deterministic testability).
3. Current code where it reveals stable domain vocabulary or already-landed behavior worth keeping.
4. README and AGENTS for product goals, non-goals, and implementation philosophy.
5. Transitional compatibility code only as negative evidence of what V3 must retire, not as something to immortalize.

Practical rule: if a concept appears in V2 and in code as a durable security or lifecycle invariant, keep it. If it appears only in transitional runtime glue (`ExecutionContext`, `ConnectorRuntime`, stdio JSON-RPC loops, Tokio compatibility handles), treat it as removal evidence.

### 3. What the sources say today

#### 3.1 V2 spec: strong ideas that survive

V2 already encodes a remarkably large amount of the final intended system:
- digital sovereignty, authenticated mesh, explicit authority,
- object identity, canonical serialization, checkpoints, revocation,
- zone architecture, provenance, taint, declassification/elevation,
- capability objects and tokens,
- connector state externalization, leases, migration, and offline repair,
- manifest interface hashing, network guard, supply-chain attestations,
- Tailscale identity and routing,
- audit chains, decision receipts, and conformance vectors.

These are not throwaway ideas. Most should survive.

#### 3.2 README/AGENTS: product intent and non-goals

README and AGENTS are emphatic about several things that V3 should treat as first-class truth:
- no backwards compatibility matters at this stage,
- connectors must be self-contained binaries or WASI modules,
- automation and provisioning matter,
- secrets should not persist to disk,
- secure-by-default and capability-first enforcement are mandatory,
- structured tracing, strong observability, and strong testing are project values.

However, AGENTS also documents a dependency stack that includes Tokio, reqwest, axum, hyper, tokio-tungstenite, etc. That is implementation reality for the moment, not the desired semantic center of FCP3.

#### 3.3 Code reality: transitional split-brain architecture

The current codebase proves that FCP is between two worlds.

Stable and worth keeping:
- `crates/fcp-core/src/connector.rs` already defines enduring connector vocabulary: handshake, invoke, simulate, subscribe, shutdown, archetypes, introspection, health.
- `crates/fcp-manifest/src/lib.rs` already validates interface hashes, capability declarations, network constraints, state models, and manifest structure in a way that clearly belongs in V3.

Transitional and not fit to be normative:
- `crates/fcp-async-core/src/lib.rs` defines `ExecutionContext` and a large Tokio-like compatibility surface while internally bridging to Asupersync, including a dedicated `tokio-compat-driver` thread and compatibility channels.
- `crates/fcp-sdk/src/migration.rs` explicitly frames connector migration around `ConnectorRuntime`, `request_context()`, `background_context()`, and compatibility-era retries instead of `&Cx`-first APIs.
- `crates/fcp-host/src/bin/fcp-host.rs` still launches connectors as subprocesses and speaks newline-delimited JSON-RPC over child stdin/stdout.
- many connector `src/main.rs` entrypoints, such as `connectors/slack/src/main.rs`, still run a line-based JSON-RPC loop and `runtime.block_on` each message.
- `crates/fwc/src/serve_mcp.rs` still defaults MCP transport to stdio JSON-RPC.
- `crates/fcp-sdk/Cargo.toml` and `crates/fcp-manifest/Cargo.toml` still describe themselves as `FCP2` crates.
- many connectors still center `reqwest`, and some streaming surfaces still depend on `tokio-tungstenite` or Tokio-native tests.

Conclusion: current implementation reality is useful as evidence of what must be replaced, not as the architecture V3 should describe.

#### 3.4 Asupersync: the missing normative center

Asupersync already models the execution semantics FCP3 needs:
- `Cx` as an explicit capability-bearing execution context,
- region-owned concurrency and `Scope` semantics,
- budget and deadline semantics,
- outcome severity including cancellation and panic distinction,
- cancel-correct drain/finalize/quiescence semantics,
- `AppSpec` and supervision for long-lived applications,
- deterministic `LabRuntime` for replayable and invariant-focused testing,
- migration guidance that explicitly says: thread `&Cx` early, replace detached tasks, isolate compat temporarily, then remove compat.

That is not an optional implementation detail. It is the right operational model for FCP3 itself.

### 4. Proposed V3 organizing structure

V3 should be reorganized roughly as follows:
- System thesis and scope.
- Foundational semantics: authority, zones, provenance, budgets, time, outcomes, cancellation, quiescence.
- Durable objects and evidence model.
- Host/application model.
- Connector application model and archetypes.
- Control/data/evidence plane (typed ABI, framing, streams, cancel, backpressure, drain, explain, health, checkpoint, receipts).
- Manifest compilation, policy, provisioning, secret handling.
- Durable state, leases, checkpoints, resume, and migration.
- Placement, mesh identity, Tailscale, object distribution, repair, offline operation.
- Registry and supply chain.
- Observability, explainability, audit, errors.
- Conformance and verification.
- Appendices with schemas/CDDL, golden vectors, connector patterns, and test corpus definitions.

The important change is ordering. V3 should open with operational semantics and authority semantics, not with symbol mechanics alone.

### 5. Section-by-section V2 -> V3 mapping

#### V2 §1 Introduction
Disposition: keep, but rewrite completely.
- Keep: digital sovereignty, explicit authority, secure connectoring, self-owned infrastructure.
- Remove: V1 comparison framing and anything that makes V3 read like a revision note.
- Rewrite: introduce FCP as a secure connector operating system whose execution model, evidence model, and mesh placement model are one coherent system.

#### V2 §2 Foundational Axioms
Disposition: keep and strengthen.
- Keep: Universal Fungibility, Authenticated Mesh, Explicit Authority.
- Add as equally foundational runtime truth: execution is capability-scoped, region-owned, budgeted, and closes to quiescence.
- Recommendation: either add an explicit execution axiom or encode it as part of foundational semantics immediately after the original three axioms.

#### V2 §3 Foundational Primitives
Disposition: keep and extend.
- Keep: `ObjectId`, `EpochId`, `SchemaId`, `ZoneId`, canonical serialization, `ObjectHeader`, `ZoneCheckpoint`.
- Add: explicit runtime and evidence primitives such as budget, deadline/time semantics, outcome classes, region/app identity, checkpoint/resume evidence, and durable receipt references where required.
- Rule: objects remain foundational, but runtime semantics become equally first-class.

#### V2 §4 Symbol Layer
Disposition: keep, but narrow and reposition.
- Keep: symbol/object distribution, frame limits, multipath delivery, reconstruction, offline resilience.
- Reframe: symbols support durable object transport, large payload distribution, repair, pre-staging, and offline recovery.
- Do not let this section force every live control interaction to be explained as a mesh object first. V3 should allow a typed live ABI for invocation and streaming while still promoting durable artifacts to content-addressed objects when audit/replay/resume requires it.

#### V2 §5 Zone Architecture
Disposition: keep largely intact.
- Keep: zone hierarchy, zone definition/policy, confidentiality and integrity flow rules, Tailscale ACL mapping, zone key handling.
- Tighten: zone semantics must flow into runtime contexts, manifests, evidence, and placement decisions, not just request metadata.

#### V2 §6 Provenance and Taint Tracking
Disposition: keep and bind more tightly to execution.
- Keep: provenance model, taint propagation, approval/elevation/declassification semantics.
- Expand: provenance and taint must be visible in request contexts, outputs, receipts, decision evidence, and explain surfaces.
- Ensure V3 makes sanitization, taint reduction, and evidence references operationally usable.

#### V2 §7 Capability System
Disposition: keep and deepen.
- Keep: capability taxonomy, capability objects/tokens, placement policy, role bundles, egress proxy credential injection.
- Reframe: capabilities are not just tokens presented to a host; they narrow what an execution context and a connector app instance may do.
- V3 should bind capability semantics directly to `Cx`/service boundaries and manifest compilation.

#### V2 §8 Mesh Architecture
Disposition: keep, but merge with placement and mobility themes.
- Keep: mesh nodes, gossip/distributed state, admission control, DoS resistance.
- Reposition: this belongs alongside placement, remote execution, repair, and offline operation rather than as a parallel concept disconnected from runtime/application semantics.

#### V2 §9 Wire Protocol
Disposition: rewrite completely.
- Keep: explicit modes, typed requests/responses/events, bounded framing, deterministic encoding, interoperability focus.
- Delete from the center: any architecture that smells like ad hoc JSON-RPC request envelopes as the enduring FCP connector ABI.
- Replace with: a typed CBOR control/data/evidence plane carrying invocation, stream open/close, event delivery, flow control/credit, cancellation, drain progress, health, checkpoint, decision/evidence retrieval, and replay hooks.
- Backpressure, cancellation, loser-drain, and structured outcomes must survive the boundary as actual semantics.

#### V2 §10 Connector Model
Disposition: rewrite completely around supervision.
- Keep: connector archetypes and lifecycle concerns.
- Replace: connector as merely a sandboxed binary exposing methods over a loop.
- V3 truth: a connector is a supervised application instance bound to a zone, with declared operations/events/resources, explicit state model, execution budgets, secret/provisioning surfaces, and durable evidence obligations.
- Native binary, WASI module, and remotely placed instance become execution forms of the same connector model.

#### V2 §11 Connector Manifest
Disposition: keep and expand.
- Keep: structure, interface hash, operations/events, capability declarations, sandbox/network policy, signatures, supply chain metadata.
- Expand into an operational manifest that declares:
  - execution form and isolation mode,
  - child services/workers if relevant,
  - restart/drain behavior,
  - budget envelopes and time expectations,
  - durable state/storage class,
  - placement constraints and mobility hints,
  - provisioning and credential injection surfaces,
  - observability/explain hooks,
  - conformance vectors or test fixtures where helpful.
- Manifest becomes a compile target for the host, not just static metadata.

#### V2 §12 Automation Recipes
Disposition: keep and enlarge.
- Keep: deterministic setup/provisioning flows.
- Expand: provisioning, credential rotation, webhook/bootstrap registration, repair actions, diagnostics, and teardown/cleanup can all live here.
- This section should support the repo philosophy that user setup should be automated wherever mechanically possible.

#### V2 §13 Registry and Supply Chain
Disposition: keep strongly.
- Keep: registry-as-source-not-dependency, mesh mirroring, TUF/Sigstore-style verification, transparency log, attestations, trust thresholds.
- V3 should preserve this with minimal conceptual change.

#### V2 §14 Lifecycle Management
Disposition: keep, but rewrite around app lifecycle semantics.
- Keep: activation, updates, rollback, revocation.
- Rewrite: lifecycle must be expressed as start, supervise, drain, finalize, quiesce, restart, checkpoint, failover, and retire semantics for supervised apps.
- Host decisions and connector obligations must be explicit.

#### V2 §15 Device-Aware Execution
Disposition: keep and merge with placement compiler.
- Keep: leases, device profiles, execution planner.
- Rewrite: placement is a runtime- and manifest-driven planning problem over budgets, zone constraints, locality, availability, resource fit, and trust/policy.
- Lease semantics remain critical.

#### V2 §16 Computation Migration
Disposition: keep and sharpen.
- Keep: checkpoint, transfer, lease handoff, resume.
- Rewrite using Asupersync-native region/app semantics: migration is checkpointed transfer of durable execution state plus fenced ownership transfer, not a vague process move.
- Receipt consistency and anti-duplication semantics need to be explicit.

#### V2 §17 Security Model
Disposition: keep.
- Keep: defense in depth, source diversity, threshold secrets.
- Tighten: connect security claims to concrete runtime semantics, evidence, and conformance tests rather than leaving them as architectural aspiration.

#### V2 §18 Trust Model and Byzantine Assumptions
Disposition: keep, but prune overclaim.
- Keep: adversarial assumptions, compromised peers/devices, replay, DoS, quorum-sensitive operations.
- Only specify Byzantine or quorum math that the system truly intends to mechanize and test.
- Avoid ornate trust claims that the implementation will not validate.

#### V2 §19 Tailscale Integration
Disposition: keep strongly.
- Keep: Tailscale as identity, discovery, routing, and ACL substrate.
- Reframe: this is part of placement and authenticated remote execution, not just a side integration chapter.

#### V2 §20 RaptorQ Deep Integration
Disposition: keep capabilities, reduce ideology.
- Keep: binary distribution, epoch/event buffering, loss-tolerant object movement, repair/pre-staging value.
- Reframe: RaptorQ is a powerful substrate where it buys resilience and bandwidth aggregation. It should not dictate awkward control-plane semantics where a typed live ABI is cleaner and faster.

#### V2 §21 Offline Access
Disposition: keep and strengthen.
- Keep: offline capability, predictive pre-staging, repair.
- Expand: checkpoint-aware resume, evidence-backed replay, mirrored registry use, and durable state recovery are part of the offline story.

#### V2 §22 Agent Integration
Disposition: keep and expand.
- Keep: introspection, MCP integration.
- Expand: explainability, reason/evidence retrieval, dry-run/simulate, replay, and rich operator/developer surfaces. These are important user-facing properties, not optional ergonomics.

#### V2 §23 Observability and Audit
Disposition: keep and strengthen heavily.
- Keep: required metrics, structured logs, audit chain, decision receipts.
- Expand: trace stitching across host, connector, placement, evidence, and external I/O; detailed structured logging; explain surfaces; replay and postmortem evidence bundles.
- This section must directly support the later unit, adversarial, and e2e test program.

#### V2 §24 Error Taxonomy
Disposition: keep and deepen.
- Keep: stable codes and structured error responses.
- Expand: align with outcome classes, decision reason codes, transport reason codes, retryability, and operator-facing explain payloads.

#### V2 §25 Implementation Phases
Disposition: remove from the main spec.
- This is project planning, not enduring protocol truth.
- If any profile split survives, keep only timeless conformance profiles that matter to interoperability.
- Move rollout sequencing and roadmap material to beads/docs, not the normative specification.

#### V2 §26 Compatibility and Migration
Disposition: delete entirely.
- The project explicitly does not care about backwards compatibility.
- V3 must not spend prose budget describing bridges, translators, or coexistence modes.
- The spec should read like the end-state architecture has always been the architecture.

#### V2 §27 Conformance Requirements
Disposition: keep and strengthen.
- Keep: mandatory tests, interop tests, fuzzing/adversarial tests, CDDL/golden vectors.
- Expand hard requirements to include:
  - deterministic lab-style runtime tests,
  - unit/property tests for every normative rule set,
  - failure-injection tests for cancellation/drain/restart/lease fencing/checkpoint resume,
  - replayable e2e scripts covering real operator and developer journeys,
  - detailed structured logging and evidence capture assertions.

### 6. Appendix disposition

Appendix A (schema): keep the idea, but likely replace with V3 manifest / ABI schema and CDDL appendices.
Appendix B (RaptorQ config): keep if RaptorQ remains normative for object transport.
Appendix C (connector patterns): keep, but rewrite around supervised connector app archetypes.
Appendix D (SDK crates): keep, but rewrite around Asupersync-native SDK surfaces.
Appendix E (conformance checklist): keep and strengthen.
Appendix F (golden decision vectors): keep and expand.
Appendix G (transport priority): either integrate into ABI/placement appendices or retain as a focused appendix.

### 7. Explicit keep / reframe / delete / newly-normative lists

#### 7.1 Keep
- zone-first isolation and zone policy objects,
- provenance, taint, approval, declassification, sanitization evidence,
- capability tokens, role bundles, network guard, credential injection,
- connector archetypes and introspection concepts,
- manifest interface hashing and operation metadata,
- durable connector state, leases, checkpoints, migration, offline repair,
- registry verification, attestations, transparency logs, mirrored distribution,
- Tailscale identity/routing/ACL integration,
- audit chains, decision receipts, stable error codes,
- conformance vectors, fuzzing, adversarial testing.

#### 7.2 Reframe
- symbol-first architecture -> durable object and repair substrate serving a larger execution/evidence model,
- gateway/host language -> root supervised application and policy/placement compiler,
- connector binary/module -> connector application instance with multiple execution forms,
- lifecycle -> supervision, quiescence, restart, checkpoint, failover,
- device-aware execution -> placement and mobility compiler,
- manifest -> operational compilation artifact,
- agent integration -> operator/developer explainability and tooling surfaces.

#### 7.3 Delete or retire
- `ExecutionContext` as the semantic center,
- `ConnectorRuntime` as the connector contract center,
- `request_context()` / `background_context()` as top-level execution model vocabulary,
- newline-delimited stdio JSON-RPC as first-class FCP connector architecture,
- host<->connector interaction defined primarily as untyped `serde_json::Value` exchange,
- Tokio compatibility handles and Tokio-shaped channel wrappers as normative design,
- FCP1 comparison material,
- implementation-phase roadmap text in the spec,
- compatibility/migration sections in the spec,
- any implication that `reqwest`, `axum`, `hyper`, or `tokio-tungstenite` are part of protocol truth.

#### 7.4 Newly normative in V3
- `Cx` as the explicit execution/authority context,
- region and scope ownership semantics,
- budget algebra, deadlines, and explicit time semantics,
- outcome classes and cancel-correct drain/finalize/quiescence behavior,
- supervision trees and `AppSpec`-style application topology,
- typed control/data/evidence plane with streaming, credit, cancel, drain, and explain semantics,
- manifest compilation into runtime and placement decisions,
- durable evidence bundles and replay-friendly transcripts,
- portable execution model spanning native, WASI, and remote placement,
- deterministic lab-style verification as part of conformance,
- operator/developer explain, doctor, replay, and evidence retrieval surfaces,
- explicit receipt consistency, checkpoint-resume, and failover correctness rules.

### 8. Code evidence that should directly shape V3 writing

Use the following files as hard evidence when making drafting decisions:
- `crates/fcp-async-core/src/lib.rs`: proves the repo already wraps Asupersync, but through a broad compatibility facade (`ExecutionContext`, Tokio-like channels, compatibility runtime thread). This is transitional, not target architecture.
- `crates/fcp-sdk/src/migration.rs`: proves the current SDK still teaches connectors to think in compatibility abstractions rather than `&Cx`-native APIs.
- `crates/fcp-host/src/bin/fcp-host.rs`: proves the current host boundary is still subprocess + stdin/stdout JSON-RPC.
- `connectors/slack/src/main.rs` and many peer `connectors/*/src/main.rs`: prove connector entrypoints still execute a line-oriented JSON-RPC loop.
- `crates/fwc/src/serve_mcp.rs`: proves stdio JSON-RPC remains a default transport assumption in adjacent tooling.
- `crates/fcp-core/src/connector.rs`: proves the enduring connector vocabulary already exists and should be preserved while becoming `&Cx`-aware and supervision-aware.
- `crates/fcp-manifest/src/lib.rs`: proves manifest validation, state models, network constraints, and interface hashing are already mature and belong in V3.
- `crates/fcp-sdk/Cargo.toml` and `crates/fcp-manifest/Cargo.toml`: prove crate naming/descriptions are still explicitly FCP2-era and therefore should not influence V3 prose direction.

### 9. Spec-writing rules for bead 1.2

When drafting `FCP_Specification_V3.md`:
- Write in present tense as if the Asupersync-native model has always been the architecture.
- Do not narrate the redesign as a migration from Tokio or JSON-RPC.
- Do not preserve compatibility shims in normative prose.
- Avoid turning implementation accidents into standards.
- Open with authority and execution semantics, not with transitional transport history.
- Keep the mesh/object/symbol story, but make it serve execution, evidence, placement, and resilience.
- Make the host/connector boundary a typed, structured ABI with explicit lifecycle semantics.
- State user-visible explainability and diagnostics as protocol/runtime features, not optional CLI sugar.
- Tie every large claim to a mechanism and a test story.

### 10. Verification doctrine that V3 must carry forward

V3 must not merely describe behavior. It must describe what conformance and correctness proof look like.

Normative verification expectations to carry into the V3 rewrite:
- inline/unit tests for all local rule systems: manifest validation, capability reasoning, provenance propagation, decision/evidence generation, stable errors,
- property and fuzz tests for ABI/frame parsing, CBOR canonicalization, lease/receipt/checkpoint invariants,
- deterministic runtime tests for cancellation, loser-drain, quiescence, restart behavior, deadline/budget handling, and region cleanup,
- failure-injection tests for stale leases, revoked credentials, crash mid-drain, crash mid-checkpoint, partitioned peers, replayed frames, and hostile service behavior,
- replayable e2e scripts covering provision -> configure -> invoke -> stream -> cancel -> drain -> checkpoint -> failover -> resume -> explain -> repair -> revoke,
- detailed structured logs with stable identifiers and reason codes,
- retained evidence bundles so postmortems and conformance review can verify not just outcomes but reasons.

### 11. Final drafting recommendation

The cleanest V3 framing is:
- FCP is the secure operating model for external-service access in the Flywheel ecosystem.
- Zones, provenance, capabilities, manifests, leases, checkpoints, registry attestations, and audit receipts define what is allowed and what is durable.
- Asupersync semantics define how execution, cancellation, supervision, budgets, and deterministic verification work.
- Tailscale identity, object distribution, placement, and repair define where execution and durable state live across the mesh.

That synthesis is the real source of truth. This bead should be treated as the canonical bridge from V2/spec intent + code reality + Asupersync semantics into the V3 rewrite.","acceptance_criteria":"`flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.1` is complete when all of the following are true:
- the durable V2 ideas worth preserving are explicitly identified,
- each major V2 section has an explicit V3 disposition and rewrite direction,
- current code evidence is used to distinguish target architecture from transitional compatibility seams,
- terms/abstractions to retire are listed explicitly,
- newly normative FCP3 concepts are listed explicitly,
- spec-writing rules and conformance/test expectations are captured so `flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.2` can proceed without reopening prior planning discussion.","notes":"## Writer guidance for bead 1.2

If a drafting choice is unclear, prefer the following:
- choose the Asupersync-native end-state over compatibility-era terminology,
- choose explicit runtime semantics over vague orchestration prose,
- choose typed ABI semantics over JSON-RPC-ish flexibility,
- choose durable evidence and replayability over optimistic assumptions,
- choose operator/developer explainability over hidden policy decisions,
- choose self-contained, automatable provisioning and secret handling over manual setup.

Two cautions:
- Do not let symbol/RaptorQ maximalism make the control plane awkward. Use durable objects where they buy resilience, replay, checkpointing, distribution, or audit. Use a live typed ABI where it buys clarity and speed.
- Do not underspec testing. The project explicitly wants comprehensive unit coverage, adversarial coverage, replayable e2e scripts, and rich logging/evidence. V3 should make that feel inevitable rather than aspirational.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:53:50.423300Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:34:12.980198Z","closed_at":"2026-03-09T21:34:12.980120Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","fcp3","spec"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:53:50.423300Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.2","title":"[FCP3.SPEC] Rewrite the normative V3 body around execution model, host, connector, ABI, manifest, and policy truth","description":"## Goal
Draft the main body of `FCP_Specification_V3.md` so it presents the Asupersync-native model as the architecture of record.

## Scope
- rewrite introduction, axioms, terminology, and foundational primitives where needed
- add explicit execution model sections covering runtime context, regions, supervision, budgets, outcomes, drains, and quiescence
- rewrite connector model, host model, ABI, manifests, policy, and placement sections to assume supervised apps and framed transports
- preserve the strongest security and mesh ideas from V2 where still valid, but rewrite them so they compose with the new operational model rather than sit beside it

## Writing rules
- do not describe V3 as a migration from V2 inside the normative text
- do not preserve compatibility-oriented language for legacy surfaces we intend to delete
- write as if the system was always intended to work this way

## Acceptance criteria
The core V3 document is internally coherent and can be used as a primary reference for architecture and implementation beads.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note
This bead is part of the FCP3 re-foundation and must remain self-contained. Future implementers should not need to consult the original planning conversation to understand intent, scope, architectural posture, or why the work matters.

## No-compat rule
Do not preserve backwards compatibility, dual-stack behavior, fallback paths, or migration-era abstractions beyond the minimum temporary quarantine explicitly called out elsewhere in the graph. Once a replacement path is proven, the old path should be deleted.

## Verification and evidence mandate
- Planning/spec/design beads must explicitly define the downstream verification story rather than hand-wave it.
- Implementation beads are not complete without focused unit/property tests, negative/failure-path coverage, and where relevant failure-injection coverage for cancellation, drain, restart, timeout, backpressure, failover, and recovery behavior.
- Host- or runtime-crossing changes must include end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logging, timestamps, digests/ids, receipts or health outcomes, and clear replay instructions.
- Evidence must be redaction-safe, easy to inspect, and sufficient for a future engineer to diagnose failures without re-deriving the intended behavior.","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:53:50.571328Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:41:06.335415Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fcp3","spec","writing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:53:50.571328Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:03.785836Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

The current file now reincorporates a large amount of detailed V2 material: canonical identifier rules, deterministic CBOR/signature behavior, object placement and HPKE sealing, zone-to-Tailscale mapping, deeper provenance and capability structures, richer FCPC/FCPS semantics, stronger manifest/sandbox detail, migration and repair mechanics, expanded supply-chain policy, richer error/reason-code catalogs, and multiple detailed appendices.

Important: this bead remains in progress because the user explicitly wants V3 to reach V2-scale detail, not merely V3-shape coherence.","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:53:50.571328Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:55:11.433561Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fcp3","spec","writing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:53:50.571328Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.2","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:03.785836Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.3","title":"[FCP3.SPEC] Refresh appendices, examples, conformance checklist, and implementation phases for V3-only reality","description":"## Goal
Refresh the appendices, examples, conformance checklist, implementation phases, and supporting examples so they match FCP3-only reality.

## Scope
- replace or rewrite outdated examples that still assume newline JSON-RPC or compatibility-first host/connector relationships
- rewrite conformance checklists and phased implementation guidance around the new architecture
- add appendix material for connector archetypes, manifest examples, ABI examples, supervision patterns, and testing expectations
- eliminate sections whose only purpose was compatibility or migration narration

## Acceptance criteria
An implementer reading the appendices does not get subtly pulled back toward V2.5 design assumptions.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note
This bead is part of the FCP3 re-foundation and must remain self-contained. Future implementers should not need to consult the original planning conversation to understand intent, scope, architectural posture, or why the work matters.

## No-compat rule
Do not preserve backwards compatibility, dual-stack behavior, fallback paths, or migration-era abstractions beyond the minimum temporary quarantine explicitly called out elsewhere in the graph. Once a replacement path is proven, the old path should be deleted.

## Verification and evidence mandate
- Planning/spec/design beads must explicitly define the downstream verification story rather than hand-wave it.
- Implementation beads are not complete without focused unit/property tests, negative/failure-path coverage, and where relevant failure-injection coverage for cancellation, drain, restart, timeout, backpressure, failover, and recovery behavior.
- Host- or runtime-crossing changes must include end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logging, timestamps, digests/ids, receipts or health outcomes, and clear replay instructions.
- Evidence must be redaction-safe, easy to inspect, and sufficient for a future engineer to diagnose failures without re-deriving the intended behavior.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:53:50.708002Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:05:20.641719Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["appendix","conformance","fcp3","spec"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.3","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:53:50.708002Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.3","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:03.920768Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","title":"[FCP3] Pilot vertical slices for representative connector archetypes","description":"# [FCP3] Pilot Vertical Slices

## Goal
Prove the new model with representative end-to-end connector slices before bulk migration or destructive cutover.

## Why this exists
Architectural elegance on paper is not enough. The redesign must be exercised through concrete connector archetypes so we do not accidentally optimize for only one style of connector or quietly skip operator-facing realities like provisioning, health, explainability, restart, and replay.

## Scope
- one request-response vertical slice
- one streaming vertical slice
- one polling or webhook or other stateful/background vertical slice
- one explicit operator and developer journey scenario pack that spans provisioning, explanation, diagnosis, repair, and replay
- shared expectations for unit tests, host-backed integration tests, replayable e2e scripts, and detailed structured evidence across every slice

## Deliverables
- representative reference connectors that validate the FCP3 runtime, host, ABI, SDK, manifest, policy, and placement model together
- scenario bundles that show both nominal and failure behavior
- concrete proof that the redesign is usable and diagnosable, not just internally consistent

## Acceptance criteria
The pilot program leaves no major connector archetype or operator workflow unproven before cutover work begins.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note
This bead is part of the FCP3 re-foundation and must remain self-contained. Future implementers should not need to consult the original planning conversation to understand intent, scope, architectural posture, or why the work matters.

## No-compat rule
Do not preserve backwards compatibility, dual-stack behavior, fallback paths, or migration-era abstractions beyond the minimum temporary quarantine explicitly called out elsewhere in the graph. Once a replacement path is proven, the old path should be deleted.

## Verification and evidence mandate
- Planning/spec/design beads must explicitly define the downstream verification story rather than hand-wave it.
- Implementation beads are not complete without focused unit/property tests, negative/failure-path coverage, and where relevant failure-injection coverage for cancellation, drain, restart, timeout, backpressure, failover, and recovery behavior.
- Host- or runtime-crossing changes must include end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logging, timestamps, digests/ids, receipts or health outcomes, and clear replay instructions.
- Evidence must be redaction-safe, easy to inspect, and sufficient for a future engineer to diagnose failures without re-deriving the intended behavior.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:52:52.872918Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:14:18.743927Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connectors","fcp3","pilot"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:52:52.872918Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:49.041565Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.14","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:04:52.452429Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:02.971920Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:03.110288Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.8","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:03.248307Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:02.834673Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","title":"[FCP3.PILOT] Native request-response connector vertical slice end-to-end","description":"## Goal
Deliver the first end-to-end request-response connector slice on the FCP3 runtime, host, ABI, SDK, manifest, and policy model.

## Why this exists
This is the simplest and most common connector archetype, so it should establish the baseline shape of invoke, simulate, receipts, health, approval, and error handling under the new architecture.

## Scope
- one representative request-response connector implemented against the FCP3 stack
- invoke and simulate flows with explicit authority, budget, and provenance handling
- receipts, health, self-check, and state exposure suitable for operators and conformance tests
- negative cases for denied capability, exhausted budget, timeout, cancellation, and transport-level failure

## Deliverables
- focused unit and property tests for request lifecycle and outcome semantics
- host-backed integration tests across nominal and failure paths
- replayable end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logs and evidence bundles

## Acceptance criteria
A future migration wave for low-state RPC-style connectors can treat this bead as the canonical proof that FCP3 handles ordinary connector work cleanly, safely, and observably.","notes":"## FCP3 execution note
This bead is part of the FCP3 re-foundation and must remain self-contained. Future implementers should not need to consult the original planning conversation to understand intent, scope, architectural posture, or why the work matters.

## No-compat rule
Do not preserve backwards compatibility, dual-stack behavior, fallback paths, or migration-era abstractions beyond the minimum temporary quarantine explicitly called out elsewhere in the graph. Once a replacement path is proven, the old path should be deleted.

## Verification and evidence mandate
- Planning/spec/design beads must explicitly define the downstream verification story rather than hand-wave it.
- Implementation beads are not complete without focused unit/property tests, negative/failure-path coverage, and where relevant failure-injection coverage for cancellation, drain, restart, timeout, backpressure, failover, and recovery behavior.
- Host- or runtime-crossing changes must include end-to-end scripts with detailed structured logging, timestamps, digests/ids, receipts or health outcomes, and clear replay instructions.
- Evidence must be redaction-safe, easy to inspect, and sufficient for a future engineer to diagnose failures without re-deriving the intended behavior.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:54:43.608051Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-09T21:14:25.787514Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fcp3","pilot","request-response"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:54:43.608051Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.13.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:04:52.022885Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.14.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:04:54.527437Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.5.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:08.939823Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.6.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:08.802834Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.6.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T21:04:54.113054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.8.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:09.076328Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.10.1","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-9syku.9.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-09T20:56:09.211930Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
